masthead.dek: Ten questions, ten different ways to measure Croatia — coast and islands, peaks and rivers, parks and borders. Narrow the field, place your bet, then tap to check yourself.
masthead.footer_html: Ten questions · ten distinct dimensions (100% unique) · sources noted per question.

questions.q: Croatia's biggest city sits inland. Which city is the largest one that sits right on the seacoast?
questions.detail: About 160,000 people (2021) — Croatia's largest coastal city and its second-largest city overall.
questions.why: Zagreb, the inland capital, has roughly 770,000 people, but once you set the capital aside, Split clearly tops the coast — well ahead of the next seaside city.
questions.more: The next-largest coastal city, Rijeka, has about 108,000 people — roughly two-thirds of Split's size.
questions.source: Croatian Bureau of Statistics, 2021 census (Split 160,577; Rijeka 108,622).

questions.q: Of all the islands along Croatia's coast, which one has the most people living on it year-round?
questions.detail: About 19,900 residents (2021) — the most populous Adriatic island.
questions.why: Krk is joined to the mainland by a bridge and sits right by the city of Rijeka, which helps it stay lived-in all year — more than any other Croatian island.
questions.more: Korčula, farther south, is next with around 15,500 people; Brač and Hvar follow.

questions.q: Which mountain holds the highest peak found anywhere in Croatia?
questions.detail: Its summit, Sinjal, reaches 1,831 meters (6,007 feet).
questions.why: Dinara rises on the border with Bosnia and Herzegovina and out-tops every coastal range; the whole Dinaric Alps mountain system takes its name from it.
questions.more: The next-highest summits — on Biokovo (1,762 m) and Velebit (1,757 m) — fall short by roughly 70 meters.
questions.source: Standard geographic references; Dinara / Sinjal peak 1,831 m.

questions.q: Which river runs the longest distance across Croatia, counting only the part inside the country?
questions.detail: About 562 km of its course lies within Croatia — the longest stretch of any river there.
questions.why: The Sava flows past Zagreb on its way toward the Danube; inside Croatia it more than doubles the in-country length of the next river.
questions.more: The Drava runs about 305 km inside Croatia. By a stricter rule — the longest river lying *entirely* within Croatia — the answer flips to the Kupa (296 km).
questions.source: Croatian geographic references; Sava 562 km in-country, Drava 305 km, Kupa 296 km.

questions.q: Croatia touches five countries by land. Which neighbor does it share its longest land border with?
questions.detail: Their shared boundary runs roughly 930 km — Croatia's longest land border by far.
questions.why: Croatia bends in a thin crescent that wraps around Bosnia and Herzegovina, so the two trace a long, winding line together — far longer than Croatia's border with Slovenia, its second-longest.
questions.more: Croatia's border with Slovenia is roughly 600 km; its shortest land border, with Montenegro, is only about 25 km.
questions.source: Geography of Croatia; Croatia–Bosnia and Herzegovina border ~932 km.

questions.q: Croatia has eight national parks. Which one draws the most visitors each year?
questions.detail: More than 1.5 million visitors a year — Croatia's most-visited national park.
questions.why: Its staircase of terraced lakes and waterfalls made it one of the country's first UNESCO World Heritage sites (1979), and it pulls in more people than any other park.
questions.more: Krka, also famous for its waterfalls, is the runner-up with close to 1 million visitors a year.
questions.source: Plitvice Lakes National Park visitor figures; UNESCO World Heritage listing (1979).

questions.q: Which is the largest natural lake in Croatia?
questions.detail: About 30 km² of surface, near the town of Biograd on the Dalmatian coast.
questions.why: Even as the country's biggest natural lake, it is strikingly shallow — only about 2 to 6 meters deep — and sits just behind the shoreline as a refuge for wintering birds.
questions.more: Bigger bodies of water exist in Croatia, but they are man-made reservoirs such as Lake Peruča — not natural lakes.
questions.source: Vransko Jezero Nature Park; lake surface ~30 km².

questions.q: Which of Croatia's well-known cities lies the farthest south?
questions.detail: At about 42.6°N, the southernmost of Croatia's major cities.
questions.why: Dubrovnik sits at the far southern tip of the coast — so far that a short strip of Bosnian land at Neum cuts it off from the rest of the country.
questions.more: For years you had to cross into Bosnia and Herzegovina and back to drive there from the rest of Croatia — until the Pelješac Bridge opened in 2022 to skip the crossing.

questions.q: Croatia is split into 20 counties plus its capital. Which county covers the most land, yet has the fewest people of them all?
questions.detail: About 5,350 km² — the largest county by area and the smallest by population (~43,000).
questions.why: It's the rugged, forested interior holding the Velebit mountains, so it looms large on the map but is very thinly settled — only around 8 people per km².
questions.more: It contains both Plitvice Lakes and Northern Velebit national parks, yet has fewer residents than a single mid-size city.
questions.source: Croatian Bureau of Statistics; Lika-Senj 5,353 km², population 42,748 (2021).

questions.q: Croatia's northwest is taken up by one big, roughly triangular peninsula — also the largest in the whole Adriatic Sea. Which peninsula is it?
questions.detail: About 3,600 km², with roughly 90% of it in Croatia and small parts in Slovenia and Italy.
questions.why: Istria is the Adriatic's largest peninsula; its green, hilly interior of vineyards and stone hilltop towns has earned it the nickname 'Croatian Tuscany.'
questions.more: Croatia's next major peninsula, Pelješac in the south, is far smaller at roughly 350 km².
questions.source: Istria regional data; peninsula area ~3,600 km² (~90% in Croatia).
